When things get too complex or overwhelming, research shows that our minds look for ways to simplify the work and take decision-making shortcuts. Actually, this benefits the ethical persuader. We now live in a more complex environment than ever before. Just think about the items we use now that our parents or grandparents couldn't dream of. Think of all the choices and decisions thrust upon us that they never had to deal with. The futurists of 50 years ago thought that by now we were supposed to be a culture being taken care of by machines, leaving us with endless leisure time and no worries. But in reality, these technologies have overloaded us with information and made our work more complex. So we need ways of simplifying our world. Crucially, the seven principles of influence offer people those simplifying ways to choose. They form what we call decision triggers, or single, reliable pieces of information that generally serve to guide people in making their decisions. Need a concrete example of how powerful a simple decision trigger can be? Let's look at an instance of how a chain of grocery stores found a simple decision trigger to promote sales. Occasionally, the managers of these stores attach signs marked every day low price in front of randomly selected brands. The prices of the products remain the same as before. But how much do you think sales increased? Although the prices hadn't changed, sales of the brands labeled every day low price increased by 100%. What could account for this effect? Researchers found that it was that the every day low price sign looked enough like a sale sign, which triggered people's automatic tendency to decide for it. They didn't process the situation any more deeply than this kind of sign usually indicates a bargain. As a result, sales doubled again. Small change, big effect.
